question: If the temperature is increased during the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid, the rate usually increases because

options.B: more particles now have enough energy to react successfully

answer_text: more particles now have enough energy to react successfully

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why heating speeds up the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: According to collision theory, particles must collide with enough energy to overcome the activation energy barrier.
steps: When temperature rises, the average kinetic energy of particles increases.
That means particles move faster and collide more often.
It also means a larger fraction of collisions now has enough energy to be effective.
Therefore the rate of the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid increases because more particles can react successfully.
explanation.final_answer: B. more particles now have enough energy to react successfully
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating makes more collisions energetic enough to lead to reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In magnesium and hydrochloric acid, the activation energy itself on adding heat

options.C: does not automatically change, but more particles can overcome it
options.D: becomes equal to the enthalpy change

answer_text: does not automatically change, but more particles can overcome it

explanation.question_restate: We are to decide what heating does to activation energy in magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Temperature changes the energy distribution of particles, not the value of the activation energy barrier itself.
steps: The activation energy is a characteristic barrier for a given reaction pathway.
Heating does not by itself remove that barrier.
Instead, more particles now have energies equal to or above that barrier.
So the reaction becomes faster even though the activation energy itself does not automatically change.
explanation.final_answer: C. does not automatically change, but more particles can overcome it
explanation.simple_explanation: Heating does not usually change the barrier itself; it only helps more particles get over it. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If two identical experiments involving magnesium and hydrochloric acid are run at 25 °C and 45 °C, the faster one will usually be the experiment at 45 °C because

options.A: particles have less kinetic energy at 45 °C
options.B: collision frequency and effectiveness are greater at 45 °C

answer_text: collision frequency and effectiveness are greater at 45 °C

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the rate of magnesium and hydrochloric acid at two temperatures.
explanation.principle: Raising temperature increases kinetic energy and the number of effective collisions.
steps: At 45 °C, particles move faster than at 25 °C.
So they collide more often and with greater energy.
This increases the number of successful collisions per second.
Therefore the experiment at 45 °C is usually faster.
explanation.final_answer: B. collision frequency and effectiveness are greater at 45 °C
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher temperature gives more frequent and more energetic collisions. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The main reason a graph of rate against temperature rises for magnesium and hydrochloric acid is that

options.B: a greater fraction of particles is above the activation energy
options.C: all particles now have the same energy

answer_text: a greater fraction of particles is above the activation energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the rate-temperature graph rises for magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: More particles lie beyond the activation-energy threshold as temperature increases.
steps: The Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution spreads toward higher energies at higher temperature.
So the area representing particles above the activation energy increases.
These are the particles most likely to react successfully on collision.
Therefore the rate rises because a greater fraction of particles is above the activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: B. a greater fraction of particles is above the activation energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher temperature means more particles are energetic enough to react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the reaction mixture for magnesium and hydrochloric acid is cooled, the reaction slows mainly because

options.B: particles collide less often and with less energy

answer_text: particles collide less often and with less energy

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why cooling slows the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Cooling reduces the average kinetic energy of particles.
steps: Slower particles collide less frequently.
Their collisions also have lower energy on average.
So fewer collisions are successful.
Therefore the reaction slows when the mixture is cooled.
explanation.final_answer: B. particles collide less often and with less energy
explanation.simple_explanation: Cooling lowers particle energy, so there are fewer successful collisions. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid, increasing the concentration of a reactant usually increases the rate because

options.A: there are fewer particles in the same volume
options.B: there are more reacting particles per unit volume

answer_text: there are more reacting particles per unit volume

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how concentration affects the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: A higher concentration means more particles in the same volume.
steps: When more particles are present in a given space, collisions happen more often.
That increases the chance of effective collisions.
So the reaction usually becomes faster.
Therefore increasing concentration increases the rate of magnesium and hydrochloric acid because there are more reacting particles per unit volume.
explanation.final_answer: B. there are more reacting particles per unit volume
explanation.simple_explanation: Higher concentration means more particles in the same space, so collision frequency rises. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the concentration of the acid is halved during magnesium and hydrochloric acid, the reaction rate will usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict the effect of halving acid concentration in magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Lower concentration means fewer reactant particles are available for collision.
steps: With fewer particles per unit volume, collisions occur less often.
That means fewer successful collisions each second.
So the reaction proceeds more slowly.
Therefore the rate usually decreases.
explanation.final_answer: C. decrease
explanation.simple_explanation: Dilution lowers collision frequency, so the reaction slows down. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In magnesium and hydrochloric acid, the most direct effect of using a more concentrated solution is to increase the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the immediate effect of higher concentration in magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Concentration affects how closely packed particles are in solution.
steps: When particles are more closely packed, they collide more often.
This raises the number of collisions per second.
Because of that, the rate usually increases.
Therefore the most direct effect is to increase the collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: A. collision frequency
explanation.simple_explanation: More concentrated solutions give more collisions each second. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If two flasks contain the same reactants for magnesium and hydrochloric acid but one flask is more dilute, the dilute flask will generally show

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the rates in concentrated and dilute conditions for magnesium and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Dilution reduces the number of reacting particles in each unit volume.
steps: Fewer particles are available for collision in the dilute flask.
So there are fewer effective collisions each second.
This lowers the rate of reaction.
Therefore the dilute flask generally shows a slower reaction.
explanation.final_answer: B. a slower reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: Dilution means fewer collisions per second, so the reaction is slower. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

Therefore the acid particles strike the solid surface more frequently.

steps: A catalyst opens a different route for the reaction.

question: The reaction involving silver chloride left in sunlight is faster in light because

options.A: light can supply energy to start or speed the reaction
options.B: light always lowers the activation energy chemically

answer_text: light can supply energy to start or speed the reaction

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the effect of light in silver chloride left in sunlight.
explanation.principle: Some reactions depend on absorbed light energy to begin or speed up.
steps: Some reactions absorb light energy directly.
That energy may break bonds or create reactive species.
These reactive species then react more readily.
Therefore the reaction involving silver chloride left in sunlight is faster in light because light can supply energy to start or speed the reaction.
explanation.final_answer: A. light can supply energy to start or speed the reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: Light can start or speed some reactions by supplying useful energy. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: A reaction accelerated by light, such as silver chloride left in sunlight, is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the effect of light in silver chloride left in sunlight.
explanation.principle: Some reactions depend on absorbed light energy to begin or speed up.
steps: The term 'photochemical' is used for reactions driven or accelerated by light.
In such reactions, light energy is involved in producing reactive changes.
Therefore a reaction such as silver chloride left in sunlight is called a photochemical reaction.
This distinguishes it from reactions driven mainly by heat or electricity.
explanation.final_answer: B. a photochemical reaction
explanation.simple_explanation: Light can start or speed some reactions by supplying useful energy. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: In sodium thiosulphate experiment set 1, the cross disappears sooner when the mixture is warmer because

options.C: the rate of sulfur formation is greater at higher temperature
options.D: the flask becomes opaque before mixing

answer_text: the rate of sulfur formation is greater at higher temperature

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why warming the sodium thiosulphate and hydrochloric acid mixture makes the cross disappear sooner.
explanation.principle: Higher temperature increases the number of effective collisions between reacting particles.
steps: When the mixture is warmer, reacting particles move faster.
This leads to more frequent and more energetic collisions.
Sulfur is formed more quickly, so the cloudiness develops sooner.
Therefore the cross disappears sooner because the rate of sulfur formation is greater at higher temperature.
explanation.final_answer: C. the rate of sulfur formation is greater at higher temperature
explanation.simple_explanation: Warming the mixture makes sulfur form faster, so the cross disappears sooner. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: If the acid concentration is reduced in sodium thiosulphate trial 1, the observed time for the cross to disappear will

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict how reducing acid concentration affects the disappearing-cross time.
explanation.principle: Lower concentration reduces collision frequency and slows sulfur formation.
steps: Dilution means fewer acid particles are present in each unit volume.
So the reaction proceeds more slowly.
Less sulfur is formed each second, so the cloudiness builds up more slowly.
Therefore the time for the cross to disappear will increase.
explanation.final_answer: B. increase
explanation.simple_explanation: A slower reaction takes longer to hide the cross. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: In sodium thiosulphate investigation 1, the cloudiness is due to the formation of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the substance responsible for the cloudiness in the sodium thiosulphate experiment.
explanation.principle: The reaction produces fine particles of sulfur.
steps: These sulfur particles remain suspended in the liquid for a time.
They scatter light and make the mixture look cloudy.
That cloudiness is what hides the black cross beneath the flask.
Therefore the cloudiness is due to sulfur.
explanation.final_answer: B. sulfur
explanation.simple_explanation: Tiny sulfur particles make the solution cloudy. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and sodium thiosulphate
question: Rate in sodium thiosulphate trial 1 is commonly estimated using

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the common rate estimate used in the sodium thiosulphate disappearing-cross experiment.
explanation.principle: The same visual stage is reached when the cross disappears, so shorter time means faster reaction.
steps: If the reaction is very fast, the required cloudiness is reached quickly.
If it is slow, the cloudiness takes longer to develop.
Because rate and time move in opposite directions, 1/time is often used as an estimate of rate.
Therefore the common rate estimate is 1/time.
explanation.final_answer: A. 1/time
explanation.simple_explanation: Short time means high rate, so 1/time is used. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: In magnesium–HCl experiment 1, the gas collected is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas produced when magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: A metal reacting with dilute acid usually gives a salt and hydrogen gas.
steps: Magnesium reacts with hydrochloric acid to form magnesium chloride.
Hydrogen ions from the acid gain electrons overall and form hydrogen gas.
So the gas given off is hydrogen.
Therefore the gas collected is H₂.
explanation.final_answer: B. H₂
explanation.simple_explanation: Magnesium displaces hydrogen from the acid, forming hydrogen gas. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: If the hydrochloric acid is more concentrated in magnesium–HCl experiment 1, the hydrogen is produced

explanation.question_restate: We are to predict how increasing acid concentration affects hydrogen evolution in the magnesium and HCl reaction.
explanation.principle: More concentrated acid gives more acid particles per unit volume.
steps: This increases the number of collisions between acid particles and the magnesium surface.
More successful collisions per second mean faster reaction.
So hydrogen is formed more quickly.
Therefore hydrogen is produced more quickly.
explanation.final_answer: C. more quickly
explanation.simple_explanation: More concentrated acid makes the metal react faster, so hydrogen appears more quickly. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: Mg and HCl
question: A suitable way to compare rates in magnesium–HCl experiment 1 is to measure

options.A: volume of H₂ produced in a fixed time

answer_text: volume of H₂ produced in a fixed time

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a practical way to compare rates in the magnesium and hydrochloric acid reaction.
explanation.principle: The reaction forms a gas, so gas volume is a useful measurable quantity.
steps: If the reaction is fast, more hydrogen is produced in the same amount of time.
If the reaction is slow, less hydrogen is produced in that time.
So measuring gas volume in a fixed time gives a direct comparison of rate.
Therefore volume of H₂ produced in a fixed time is suitable.
explanation.final_answer: A. volume of H₂ produced in a fixed time
explanation.simple_explanation: Since hydrogen gas is produced, gas volume is a convenient way to compare rates. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: In marble–HCl experiment 1, the gas evolved is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas evolved when marble reacts with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Marble is mainly calcium trioxocarbonate(IV), and acids react with carbonates to produce carbon dioxide.
steps: The reaction forms calcium chloride, water and carbon dioxide.
The gaseous product is carbon dioxide.
This gas can be collected and measured during the experiment.
Therefore the gas evolved is CO₂.
explanation.final_answer: B. CO₂
explanation.simple_explanation: Acid plus carbonate gives carbon dioxide gas. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: If powdered marble is used instead of chips in marble–HCl experiment 1, the rate increases because

options.B: the surface area exposed to acid is larger

answer_text: the surface area exposed to acid is larger

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why powdered marble reacts faster than chips.
explanation.principle: A larger surface area allows more acid particles to strike the solid each second.
steps: Powdered marble exposes much more total surface than chips of the same mass.
That means more contact points between acid and solid carbonate.
So more successful collisions happen each second.
Therefore the rate increases because the surface area exposed to acid is larger.
explanation.final_answer: B. the surface area exposed to acid is larger
explanation.simple_explanation: More exposed solid surface means more collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: A practical way to follow the rate in marble–HCl experiment 1 is to record

options.A: the volume of CO₂ against time
options.D: the name of the salt formed

answer_text: the volume of CO₂ against time

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a practical measurement for the rate of marble reacting with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: The reaction produces carbon dioxide gas, so gas volume is a natural quantity to follow.
steps: If the reaction is fast, CO₂ volume rises quickly.
If it is slow, the volume rises more gradually.
Plotting or recording gas volume against time therefore allows the rate to be studied.
So the correct answer is the volume of CO₂ against time.
explanation.final_answer: A. the volume of CO₂ against time
explanation.simple_explanation: Gas volume is a good rate measure because carbon dioxide is produced as the reaction proceeds. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a practical measurement for the rate of marble reacting with hydrochloric acid.

If it is slow, the volume rises more gradually.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: In marble–HCl experiment 8, the gas evolved is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas evolved when marble reacts with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Marble is mainly calcium trioxocarbonate(IV), and acids react with carbonates to produce carbon dioxide.
steps: The reaction forms calcium chloride, water and carbon dioxide.
The gaseous product is carbon dioxide.
This gas can be collected and measured during the experiment.
Therefore the gas evolved is CO₂.
explanation.final_answer: B. CO₂
explanation.simple_explanation: Acid plus carbonate gives carbon dioxide gas. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: HCl and marble
question: If powdered marble is used instead of chips in marble–HCl experiment 8, the rate increases because

options.B: the surface area exposed to acid is larger

answer_text: the surface area exposed to acid is larger

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why powdered marble reacts faster than chips.
explanation.principle: A larger surface area allows more acid particles to strike the solid each second.
steps: Powdered marble exposes much more total surface than chips of the same mass.
That means more contact points between acid and solid carbonate.
So more successful collisions happen each second.
Therefore the rate increases because the surface area exposed to acid is larger.
explanation.final_answer: B. the surface area exposed to acid is larger
explanation.simple_explanation: More exposed solid surface means more collisions and a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: In iodine clock run 1, the blue-black colour appears when

options.A: free iodine finally accumulates and reacts with starch

answer_text: free iodine finally accumulates and reacts with starch

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the moment when the blue-black colour appears in the iodine clock reaction.
explanation.principle: The starch colour appears only when free iodine is present in sufficient amount.
steps: At first, iodine formed is removed by another substance in the mixture.
After that substance is used up, iodine begins to accumulate.
This iodine reacts with starch to give the blue-black colour.
Therefore the colour appears when free iodine finally accumulates and reacts with starch.
explanation.final_answer: A. free iodine finally accumulates and reacts with starch
explanation.simple_explanation: The sudden colour appears when iodine is no longer being removed and can react with starch. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: If iodine clock run 1 gives a shorter clock time after a change in conditions, the reaction has become

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret what a shorter clock time means in the iodine clock reaction.
explanation.principle: The clock time is the delay before the visible endpoint is reached.
steps: A shorter delay means the system reached the endpoint sooner.
That can only happen if the reaction sequence is proceeding more quickly.
So the reaction rate has increased.
Therefore the reaction has become faster.
explanation.final_answer: B. faster
explanation.simple_explanation: Less waiting time before the colour appears means a faster reaction. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Typical reaction examples: iodine clock reaction
question: The starch in iodine clock run 1 is used mainly to

options.B: show the presence of iodine by colour change

answer_text: show the presence of iodine by colour change

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the purpose of starch in the iodine clock experiment.
explanation.principle: Starch forms a characteristic blue-black complex with iodine.
steps: This makes the appearance of iodine very easy to observe.
Because of that, starch acts as an indicator of iodine presence.
It does not serve mainly as a catalyst or reactant here.
Therefore starch is used to show the presence of iodine by colour change.
explanation.final_answer: B. show the presence of iodine by colour change
explanation.simple_explanation: Starch is added so iodine can be seen clearly by its blue-black colour. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: On a graph of volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl, in rate-curve set 1, the slope at any given point represents the

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret the slope on a graph of ('volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl', 'product').
explanation.principle: The slope of a graph shows how fast the vertical quantity changes with time.
steps: If the measured quantity changes quickly, the slope is steep.
If it changes slowly, the slope is gentle.
In rate studies, that slope therefore represents the reaction rate at that instant.
So the slope at any given point represents the reaction rate at that point.
explanation.final_answer: A. reaction rate at that point
explanation.simple_explanation: The steeper the graph at a point, the faster the reaction at that moment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the curve of volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl becomes flatter with time in rate-curve set 1, this usually means that

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain what a flatter curve means on a graph of ('volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl', 'product').
explanation.principle: A flatter curve means the measured quantity is changing more slowly with time.
steps: As reactions proceed, reactants are often used up.
This lowers collision frequency and the reaction rate falls.
A lower rate produces a less steep graph.
Therefore a flatter curve usually means the reaction rate is decreasing.
explanation.final_answer: A. the reaction rate is decreasing
explanation.simple_explanation: A flatter graph means the measured change is happening more slowly, so the rate is lower. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: At the beginning of the graph of volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl in rate-curve set 1, the rate is often greatest because

options.D: the catalyst has been used up

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the beginning of the graph of ('volume of H₂ against time for Mg and HCl', 'product') is often steepest.
explanation.principle: At the start of a reaction, reactant amounts are usually at their maximum.
steps: More reactant particles means more frequent collisions.
That leads to a high number of successful collisions each second.
So the initial rate is often the greatest.
Therefore the rate is often greatest at the beginning because reactant concentration is highest.

explanation.principle: The slope of a graph shows how fast the vertical quantity changes with time.
steps: If the measured quantity changes quickly, the slope is steep.
If it changes slowly, the slope is gentle.
In rate studies, that slope therefore represents the reaction rate at that instant.
So the slope at any given point represents the reaction rate at that point.
explanation.final_answer: A. reaction rate at that point
explanation.simple_explanation: The steeper the graph at a point, the faster the reaction at that moment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the curve of amount of product against time becomes flatter with time in rate-curve set 4, this usually means that

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain what a flatter curve means on a graph of ('amount of product against time', 'product').
explanation.principle: A flatter curve means the measured quantity is changing more slowly with time.
steps: As reactions proceed, reactants are often used up.
This lowers collision frequency and the reaction rate falls.
A lower rate produces a less steep graph.
Therefore a flatter curve usually means the reaction rate is decreasing.
explanation.final_answer: A. the reaction rate is decreasing
explanation.simple_explanation: A flatter graph means the measured change is happening more slowly, so the rate is lower. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: At the beginning of the graph of amount of product against time in rate-curve set 4, the rate is often greatest because

options.D: the catalyst has been used up

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the beginning of the graph of ('amount of product against time', 'product') is often steepest.
explanation.principle: At the start of a reaction, reactant amounts are usually at their maximum.
steps: More reactant particles means more frequent collisions.
That leads to a high number of successful collisions each second.
So the initial rate is often the greatest.
Therefore the rate is often greatest at the beginning because reactant concentration is highest.
explanation.final_answer: B. reactant concentration is highest
explanation.simple_explanation: More reactant particles are available at the start, so the rate is highest then. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If a graph of amount of product against time eventually becomes horizontal, this usually in set 4?

options.C: the temperature is rising without limit

explanation.question_restate: We are to interpret a horizontal final part of a graph of ('amount of product against time', 'product').
explanation.principle: A horizontal line means the measured quantity is no longer changing with time.
steps: If product amount stops increasing, or reactant amount stops decreasing, the reaction rate has become zero or almost zero.
This usually happens when the reaction is complete or has effectively stopped.
So the graph levels off at the end.
Therefore a horizontal section usually shows that the reaction has effectively stopped.
explanation.final_answer: A. the reaction has effectively stopped
explanation.simple_explanation: When the graph becomes horizontal, the measured quantity is no longer changing significantly. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In an uncatalyzed reaction, activation energy is best defined as the in set 1?

options.B: minimum energy needed for a successful collision

answer_text: minimum energy needed for a successful collision

explanation.question_restate: We are to analyze the idea of activation energy in an uncatalyzed reaction.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction can occur.
steps: Particles must collide before they can react.
However, not every collision causes reaction.
The colliding particles must have enough energy to get over the energy barrier.
That minimum required energy is called the activation energy.
explanation.final_answer: B. minimum energy needed for a successful collision
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the barrier particles must overcome before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: If the activation energy for a catalyzed reaction is lowered, the reaction rate at the same temperature will usually in set 1?

explanation.question_restate: We are to analyze the idea of activation energy in a catalyzed reaction.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction can occur.
steps: At the same temperature, the energy distribution of particles stays the same.
If the activation-energy barrier is lower, more particles now have enough energy to cross it.
So more collisions are successful.
Therefore the reaction rate usually increases.
explanation.final_answer: B. increase
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the barrier particles must overcome before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: The effect of a catalyst on the energy profile for the magnesium and acid reaction is to in set 1?

explanation.question_restate: We are to analyze the idea of activation energy in the magnesium and acid reaction.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction can occur.
steps: On an energy profile, the highest point represents the energy barrier.
A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with a lower barrier.
So the peak for the catalyzed route is lower.
Therefore a catalyst lowers the activation-energy peak.
explanation.final_answer: B. lower the activation-energy peak
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the barrier particles must overcome before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In collision theory, a collision in the marble and acid reaction is successful only if the particles have in set 1?

options.A: any amount of energy at all

explanation.question_restate: We are to analyze the idea of activation energy in the marble and acid reaction.
explanation.principle: Activation energy is the energy barrier that must be overcome before reaction can occur.
steps: Particles must meet for a reaction to happen.
But mere contact is not enough.
They must collide with enough energy and in a suitable arrangement.
Therefore a successful collision needs sufficient energy and proper orientation.
explanation.final_answer: B. sufficient energy and proper orientation
explanation.simple_explanation: Activation energy is the barrier particles must overcome before they can react. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: pressure for gaseous systems
question: For a gas reaction in setup P1, lowering pressure will usually

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply a basic rate-of-reaction principle to this experiment.
explanation.principle: Reaction rate depends on how often effective collisions occur and, in some cases, on how easily particles can cross the activation-energy barrier.
steps: Lower pressure means the gas particles are more spread out.
Because of this, they meet less often.
So the number of collisions per second decreases.
Therefore lowering pressure usually decreases collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: B. decrease collision frequency

explanation.principle: Reaction rate depends on how often effective collisions occur and, in some cases, on how easily particles can cross the activation-energy barrier.
steps: Lower pressure means the gas particles are more spread out.
Because of this, they meet less often.
So the number of collisions per second decreases.
Therefore lowering pressure usually decreases collision frequency.
explanation.final_answer: B. decrease collision frequency
explanation.simple_explanation: This follows directly from collision theory and the conditions of the experiment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

subtopic: Factors affecting reaction rate: surface area
question: In surface-area test S1, a powder reacts faster than a lump because the powder

options.C: always has a catalyst mixed in

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply a basic rate-of-reaction principle to this experiment.
explanation.principle: Reaction rate depends on how often effective collisions occur and, in some cases, on how easily particles can cross the activation-energy barrier.
steps: A powder exposes more of the solid to the other reactant.
That creates more contact points.
More contact points mean more collisions each second.
Therefore the powder reacts faster because it has greater exposed surface area.
explanation.final_answer: A. has greater exposed surface area
explanation.simple_explanation: This follows directly from collision theory and the conditions of the experiment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In catalyst test K1, the catalyst makes the reaction faster because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply a basic rate-of-reaction principle to this experiment.
explanation.principle: Reaction rate depends on how often effective collisions occur and, in some cases, on how easily particles can cross the activation-energy barrier.
steps: A catalyst changes the pathway followed by the reaction.
This new pathway has a lower activation energy.
So more particles can react successfully at the same temperature.
Therefore the catalyst makes the reaction faster because it provides an easier route.
explanation.final_answer: A. provides an easier route
explanation.simple_explanation: This follows directly from collision theory and the conditions of the experiment. To understand this idea better, check our "Tutorial section" for a fuller beginner-friendly lesson.

question: In graph study G12, the steeper the curve, the

options.C: smaller the volume of product always

explanation.question_restate: We are to apply a basic rate-of-reaction principle to this experiment.
explanation.principle: Reaction rate depends on how often effective collisions occur and, in some cases, on how easily particles can cross the activation-energy barrier.
steps: A steep curve means the measured quantity changes rapidly with time.
Rapid change means a high rate.
A gentle slope would indicate a slower rate.
Therefore the steeper the curve, the faster the reaction.
explanation.final_answer: B. faster the reaction
